rows.v: Cointreau, Grand Marnier, Triple Sec, Blue Curaçao, Limoncello, Grapefruit Liqueur
The backbone of countless classic cocktails — Margaritas, Sidecars, and more.
Curaçao began on a dry Caribbean island where transplanted Spanish orange trees turned bitter and inedible — but the fragrant peel became the base of the world's orange liqueurs.

groups.name: Berry & Red-Fruit Liqueurs

rows.v: Chambord, Crème de Cassis, Crème de Framboise, Maraschino, Sloe Gin
Add color and fruity depth to cocktails, or drizzle over desserts.
Maraschino is made from sour Marasca cherries crushed whole — pits and all — which is exactly where its faintly bitter, almond-like edge comes from.

groups.name_es: Licores De Bayas Y Fruta Roja

rows.v: Apricot Liqueur, Peach Schnapps, Crème de Banane, Midori (Melon), Pomegranate Liqueur
Bring fruit-forward sweetness to tiki drinks, sours, and simple highballs.
Midori means 'green' in Japanese, and the electric color is the whole point — it launched at a Studio 54 party in 1978 to fuel disco-era cocktails.
Aperol, Campari, Cynar, Fernet-Branca, Amaro Montenegro, Suze
Sip on their own before dinner, or use to add bitter complexity to cocktails.
Campari got its signature red from carmine dye made of crushed cochineal insects for over a century, until the brand finally switched to an artificial colorant.
Chartreuse, Bénédictine, Jägermeister, Drambuie, Sambuca, Pastis
Complex, herb-driven liqueurs — sip neat, use as a modifier, or finish a cocktail with a float.
Only two Carthusian monks at a time know the full recipe for Chartreuse — a blend of 130 herbs — and they're famously not allowed to travel together.
St-Germain (Elderflower), Crème de Violette, Crème de Menthe, Crème de Rose
Small amounts add aromatic lift to cocktails without overwhelming the base spirit.
St-Germain is pressed from elderflowers picked by hand in the French Alps each spring — and in the early days the blossoms were carried down the mountain on bicycles.

groups.name: Nutty & Spice Liqueurs

rows.v: Amaretto, Frangelico, Nocino, Crème de Noyaux, Fireball, Allspice Dram
Add warmth and richness to dessert cocktails, coffee drinks, and baking.
Amaretto often tastes of almond without containing a single nut — the flavor comes from apricot kernels, whose pits share the same aromatic compound as almonds.

groups.name_es: Licores De Frutos Secos Y Especias

rows.v: Kahlúa, Tia Maria, Espresso Liqueur, Crème de Cacao, Baileys Irish Cream, Advocaat
Dessert in a glass — shake into cream-based cocktails or pour over ice cream.
Baileys had to invent a way to stop cream and whiskey from separating — once they cracked that emulsion in 1974, the entire cream-liqueur category was born.
